title: Min Stack
description: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element, all in O(1) time.

examples.input: push(-2), push(0), push(-3), getMin() -> -3, pop(), top() -> 0, getMin() -> -2

hints: A regular stack gives you O(1) push/pop/top. The challenge is O(1) getMin. What changes when you push or pop?
Maintain a second stack that tracks the current minimum at each level.
When pushing, also push onto min stack: min(val, current min). When popping, pop from both stacks.
follow_ups: Can you use only one stack?

title: Daily Temperatures
description: Given an array of daily temperatures, return an array where answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after day i for a warmer temperature. If no future day is warmer, put 0.
hints: For each day, you're looking for the next greater element. What data structure helps with 'next greater' problems?
A monotonic decreasing stack stores indices of temperatures waiting for a warmer day.
Iterate through temperatures. While stack is non-empty and current temp > stack top's temp, pop and compute the difference in indices. Push current index.
follow_ups: Can you solve it without a stack?
What about finding the previous greater element?

title: Implement Queue Using Stacks
description: Implement a FIFO queue using only two stacks. The queue should support push, pop, peek, and empty operations.

examples.input: push(1), push(2), peek() -> 1, pop() -> 1, empty() -> false

hints: A stack is LIFO, a queue is FIFO. How can reversing help?
Use two stacks: one for input, one for output. Transfer from input to output when output is empty.
Push to input stack. For pop/peek, if output stack is empty, transfer all from input to output (reverses order). Then pop/peek from output.
follow_ups: Can you implement a stack using two queues?
